data.title: Capri Holdings Stock Outlook Bolstered By Likely Versace Sale - Strong Buy Reiterated
data.description:
Back in late-October of 2024, in the immediate aftermath of the U.S. Federal Trade Commission's blockage of the Tapestry (TPR) and Capri Holdings (NYSE:CPRI) merger, I published an analysis on Capri. I was one of the majority of investors who had expected the merger to clear. As I wrote in my "Comprehensive Q1 Review Of My Stock Picks + Ratings Updates" in late-March, it was surprising that the luxury retail space would be subject to protection on antitrust grounds. The risk of a single luxury goods provider being able to corner the market seems very small. Furthermore, I'm hard-pressed to understand why the FTC would even be concerned with protecting customers of luxury items (as opposed to, say, groceries, which are necessities of life).

Nonetheless, the deal was blocked, and CPRI's stock was summarily chopped in half on one mid-Autumn morning.

In my original article titled, "A Refocused Capri Holdings Looks Like A Steal Under $25", I explained why I felt CPRI was a Strong Buy opportunity, and added it to my portfolio.

In the 5-months preceding my article, not much really changed. The stock was mostly range-bound near the $20 level, and the company pretty much continued slugging away in its existing form.

The newswires have heated up here in April 2025, with the Trump Administration's April 2nd introduction of large punitive tariffs on most South-East Asian countries, and on April 9th the sudden reduction of tariffs to a baseline 10% rate (notably excluding China, however). There's also a great deal of chatter about a potential sale of Capri's Versace brand. It's a good time to reassess this holding.

Review of Original CPRI Strong Buy Thesis

In October, at ~$21/share, I was excited to add CPRI to my portfolio for many reasons, including:

  • The fact that Capri's gross margins had been holding up, suggesting that its Michael Kors, Jimmy Choo, and Versace brands were not impaired.
  • The opportunity for Capri's executive branch to refocus on managing the company, after being distracted by the impending (seemingly) Tapestry merger for ~14 months.
  • The possibility of better management of the brands by either the existing Capri executive team (primarily holdovers from Michael Kors), or else by a new set of leaders.
  • The potential to unload either the Jimmy Choo or Versace brands, or both, at a good price.
  • The possibility that merger potential would resurface, especially if Republicans won the White House. Many investors felt that the FTC was guilty of overreach during the Biden administration, especially with regards to Tapestry/Capri.
  • The fact that my portfolio previously had very little exposure to the luxury space.

At the time of my initial bullish article on CPRI, the company had posted 7 consecutive quarters of declining y/o/y sales. I applied a 1.5x EV/S multiple to the lowest Wall Street analyst estimate for fiscal 2026 revenue ($4.67 billion) and reached a target price of $45.60/share. At more than double CPRI's stock price at the time, I landed on a Strong Buy Rating.

Capri's Import Tariff Exposure

Let me uncover the first two things every investor wants to know when considering a retail stock at this moment: i) the source of manufacturing, and ii) the location of sales.

Capri Holdings Manufacturing

Most of the Versace brand's manufacturing occurs in Italy, not Asia. The story for Jimmy Choo is similar - production mainly occurs in Europe, with only a small manufacturing footprint in Asia. Critically, however, these 2 brands only represent about one-third of total Capri sales, with the remaining ~two-thirds by Michael Kors.

When it comes to Michael Kors, Capri's 10-K discloses that Kors' largest manufacturing contractor is in Asia (believed to be in Guangzhou, China), and accounts for about 14% of product volume for this brand. The company further notes that the majority of Michael Kors products are produced in Asia. Again, Kors products account for about 66% of total company sales.

Capri Holdings Location of Sales

The Americas represented nearly 55% of consolidated revenue (in USD) for Capri in 2024. That segment includes sales in Canada and Latin America. However, the geographic distribution of sales varies substantially by brand. Less than one-third of Versace and Jimmy Choo sales are to the Americas region. On the flip side, nearly two-thirds of Michael Kors revenues are located in the Americas.

In assessing the potential impact of import tariffs on Capri Holdings, we should be primarily concerned with sales within the United States of products produced in China (which as of today, April 9, carry a steep 125% import tax). As a secondary concern are U.S. sales of products manufactured in other Southeast Asian countries. The tariff rates announced by the Trump Administration on April 2nd were between 25% and 50% for most Asian countries, including 46% for Vietnam. Those tariff rates have officially been delayed (downgraded to 10%) for 90 days by the Trump administration, but nonetheless are still potentially on the radar.

Of Michael Kors' 293 retail stores in The Americas region, the company's website suggests that 239 of these (or ~82%) are located in the United States.

Potential Import Tariff Impact on Capri

With Michael Kors representing some 66% of Capri's combined sales, about 66% of Kors sales occurring in The Americas, and ~82% of Kors Americas locations in the United States, we can perform the following calculation.

% of Capri sales subject to high-rate U.S. Import tariffs on Asian countries =

66% X 66% X 82% = ~36%

Some ~36% of Capri Holdings USD revenues may be exposed to United States import tariffs on products from Asia. That assumption is unlikely to be perfectly accurate, but has a solid enough foundation to work as a realistic estimate.

Since Capri doesn't appear to own and operate its own manufacturing facilities (the company outsources production), the paper trail kind of ends before we want it to. However, numerous sources on the internet (source 1, source 2, source 3) suggest that the majority of Michael Kors product does originate in China, although there may be some production in other Asian countries like India, Vietnam and others.

For this reason, I'll ballpark Capri's USD sales exposure to China tariffs at 30%. Another estimated 6% of sales are likely exposed to U.S. tariffs on imports from other Asian countries.

We mustn't forget that the Trump Administration also implemented a 20% flat tariff on products from the EU on April 2nd, which was reduced to 10% on April 9 for at least 90 days.

With the majority of Versace and Jimmy Choo manufacturing occurring in Europe, we also need to consider that exposure as well. I'll save readers from the detailed math, but using a similar process as was done with Michael Kors, I estimate that about 10% and 7.5% of Capri's sales are exposed to U.S. import tariffs on Europe, from Jimmy Choo and Versace respectively.

Ultimately, I'd summarize Capri's U.S. import tariff exposure as follows:

(ESTIMATES ONLY) % of USD sales

current tariff rate

 possible July rate

U.S. import tariff exposure on China

 30% 125% 125%
U.S. import tariff exposure on Other Asia 6% 10% 40%*
U.S. import tariff exposure on Europe 17.5% 10% 20%
Average tariff 39.85% 43.40%

Capri Holdings' average tariff rate (on company-wide sales) could be running at about 40% right now. This is pretty significant.

Possible Versace Sale & Balance Sheet Impact

Donatella Versace, who served as Artistic Director for her namesake brand for more than 25 years (including after it was acquired by Kors in 2018) stepped down just last month. This was likely a function of Capri Holdings looking to sell the famous Italian brand, and that is now more likely than ever.

In early March, it was rumored that Prada was in discussions to acquire Versace for some USD $1.6 billion. This, of course, was prior to the introduction of the United States' unexpectedly steep tariffs.

Only about one-third of Versace sales occur in Capri's "Americas" segment, and the U.S likely accounts for only about 25% of that. Those sales are exposed to U.S. import tariffs on Europe, which were initially established at 20%. News broke early on Wednesday April 9th that Prada was still interested in Versace but was looking for an acquisition discount (below the originally rumored USD $1.6 billion). Seeking Alpha reported a potential sale price of USD $1.4 billion, and a source at the Wall Street journal tipped the probability of a deal at 50%.

With the Trump Administration's "pause" on reciprocal tariffs announced midday on April 9th, the effective rate of U.S. tariffs on goods imported from Europe appears to have been cut from 20% to 10%, at least temporarily.

In my opinion, this is likely to clinch the deal, and I expect Versace to be sold to Prada for somewhere in the neighborhood of USD $1.5 billion.

Notably, at that price Capri would be able to retire all of its long-term debt, if it wishes to. Company management might also consider conducting stock buybacks if they determine the company is undervalued. Regardless of the choice, Capri's Enterprise Value is likely to shrink to around $1.9 billion (if the sale of Versace is closed) based on a $16 CPRI share price (about 2% below April 9th's close).

Concluding Remarks and Recommendation

When I purchased shares of CPRI back nearly 6 months ago, I was hoping that company management would shake things up. So far, it's mostly been status quo for the company, and revenues have continued to bleed at a 10%-15% y/o/y rate.

Offloading Versace, which has been barely profitable and accounts for about 20% of Capri's sales, would be a really positive move in my view. Capri's North American leadership team have been poor custodians of the famous European brand. Returning Versace to an Italian operator like Prada is probably for the best, and Prada would be paying for an anticipated improvement in operating results.

Should the sale of Versace occur near the rumored price, Capri's balance sheet can be substantially improved. CPRI's Enterprise Value would decline by an estimated 45% on completion of a deal (at a $16 CPRI share price), despite only removing about ~20% from sales. This supports my existing view that Capri's 3 current brands as standalone parts are likely worth more than what the company currently is as a whole.

Unfortunately, the boost from a likely Versace sale will be mitigated by tariff risks on Capri's remaining business. The Trump Administration's sudden softening of its tariff stance doesn't much benefit Michael Kors (due to apparent reliance on China manufacturing), which would represent ~85% of Capri (on the basis of sales) if Versace is offloaded.

Thankfully, Kors has been the most profitable of the 3 brands, by far. Operating income recently stood at 18% for Kors alone (as compared to just ~13% for the combined brands). Bottom line $ profits (as a function of projected lower sales), however, seem certain to take a big hit, especially if Trump's tough stance on China tariffs remains. Unfortunately, I believe it will for the foreseeable future, as any backing down from its tariffs would likely be seen as a defeat for the so-called master negotiator.

Michael Kors revenues fell -9.3% in 2024 versus the prior year, and those sales are likely subject to further declines due to tariffs. An additional -25% decline would bring Capri's fiscal-2026 revenues (Kors + Jimmy Choo combined) to around $3.1 billion. This is far below current analyst estimates, but those estimates probably haven't been updated for tariff exposure, and will also include Versace.

In October 2024, I applied a 1.5x EV/S multiple in reaching a $45.60 target price. Even if I lower that multiple to 1x, against a -20% expected decline in Kors/Choo revenues, I reach an Enterprise Value of $3.1 billion for Capri (excluding Versace). That would represent nearly 65% upside from the adjusted-EV at a share price of $16.

Shares of CPRI were trading as low as $12.16 on Tuesday, April 8th, rallying more than $4/share or ~33% following the updated Versace sale rumors and Donald Trump's pause on most international tariffs on April 9th. While investors could default to viewing Wednesday's rally as overheated for CPRI, the business numbers suggest that the probable Versace sale could trigger a much higher valuation for Capri.

I continue to rate CPRI a Strong Buy, with a conservative price target of $26.50, assuming that the Versace sale goes through.

data.title: Container Freight Volatility Rises Amid Trade Disruptions
data.description:
By Paul Wightman

At a Glance

  • Traditional trade routes, particularly the Asia to Europe route, are experiencing more frequent disruptions
  • The container trade is projected to grow around 3.6% each year through 2028

The container freight market is undergoing significant changes that could have major implications for global trade flows.

The latest report from the UN Trade and Development (UNCTAD) agency estimated that global trade saw a record expansion to $33 trillion in 2024, an increase of 3.7% from 2023. Volatility is rising on the back of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, which is bringing risk management strategies around hedging costs to the fore.

Rising Risks in an Uncertain Market

The container market is an integral part of the overall shipping market, with over 90% of global goods shipped by sea. Of this, around 60% is transported in containers. The potential shifts in global trade are causing uncertainty in the container markets, and in many cases key shipments are being re-routed to other destinations where possible. These developments are raising questions around the cost of shipping, with the main trade lane from production centers in Asia to destinations like the U.S. or Europe being some of the most impacted due to the sheer volume of goods transported on these routes.

The softening of the spot market rates continues to challenge the long-held belief that the longer-term fixed term contracts are beneficial to the market. Declining spot rates edging toward the level of the longer-term fixed prices are prompting some major liners and freight forwarders to re-consider rates that are more closely tied to the spot markets, and indices such as the Baltic Index (FBX) represent a good reference price for this trade.

With the continued uncertainty regarding the cost of freight, more companies – from the liners to the freight forwarders and the cargo owners – are evaluating their trading strategies. Managing the cost of freight typically brings some degree of price certainty to the supply chains for moving goods from the point of production to the point of consumption. Companies are looking at what they hedge, which could contribute to the continued development of the financial markets in container freight at exchanges like CME Group.

Volatility and Disruptions Continue

The Baltic (FBX) freight indices, which are a leading industry benchmark for the trading of container freight, remains volatile across many of the trade routes. The North Asia to Europe and North Asia to the U.S. West Coast routes are two of the most volatile amid the policy shifts, disruptions in the South China Sea and re-routing away from the Suez Canal on the back of more frequent attacks on shipping off Yemen.

The latest 2024 UNCTAD report shows that there was a total of 23 million Twenty Foot Equivalent Units (TEUs) traded in 2024 between Asia and Europe, which highlights the important role that the Suez Canal plays in maritime trade.

Disruptions to the traditional trade routes, most notably in the Asia to Europe route, have also become more frequent. It is important to note that the ongoing conflict in the Middle East has contributed to a greater disruption to freight entering into and exiting from the Suez Canal, which has forced owners to take much longer diversions to arrive in Europe. Businesses that rely on imports or export to global markets have had to plan much further ahead, or in some cases, divert goods to other markets. This has all been a contributing factor to the overall volatility in the freight price.

Growth Outlook in Containers Appears Robust

The latest report from UNCTAD shows that there were 59.36 million TEUs shipped on the main East-West routes in 2024, a rise of around 5% on the prior year. The DHL Global Forwarding report for 2025 shows that on a longer-term basis, the container trade is expected to grow 3.6% on average from 2024 to 2028. In volume terms, this would mean adding a further 6.1 million TEUs of capacity per year.

The volatility in the container freight market looks set to continue considering the number of geopolitical events that are disrupting supply chains of key goods. Freight routes are being diverted, cargoes are taking longer to be delivered, and all of these factors are contributing to heightened levels of volatility. As companies along the supply chain look to hedge price risk in the market, the container freight market could see a higher level of activity in the months ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Despite New Tariffs, Under Armour Appears Poised To Rebound
data.description:
In 1989, Mars Blackmon, played by actor and director Spike Lee, became famous in a Nike commercial for telling Michael Jordan, “It’s gotta be the shoes!” Blackmon was hypothesizing why Jordan was the world’s best basketball player. Of course, Nike’s Air Jordan launched Nike’s athletic shoes into the stratosphere for popularity for the next decade.

In the shadows of Nike’s big decade came the founding of Under Armour (NYSE:UAA, NYSE:UA) in 1996. The company became wildly popular for its clothing lines in the early 2000s, seemingly able to go toe to toe with the big players in the market, like Nike. The company went public in 2005, and the stock grew by over 1000% over the next decade. Today, the value of the stock sits below its original IPO price. Many people trace the fall of the company to the commoditization of its moisture-wicking apparel, which led to massive product discounting, and its over-extension into athletic footwear, a business line where Nike was just too powerful. The company simply tried to do too much, and the stock price fell rapidly in the second half of the last decade.

The irony of this story is that I believe that footwear may be a key part of the company’s turnaround. Under Armour has been suffering from deteriorating margins and declining earnings for years, but even with the cloud of tariffs hanging overhead, 2025 may be the year that the company gets pointed back in the right direction. I rate Under Armour as a speculative buy for the next 18 months. I believe the company has learned from its past mistakes and appears ready to move ahead with the right focus for its future. Margins are improving, the company has spent the last 12 months going through some restructuring pain, and UA’s new shoe, the Echo, is one example of a spark that could light the way for a turnaround for the company.

The Return of Kevin Plank

The first step in the turnaround of the company can likely be traced back to last year, when Kevin Plank returned to the CEO position. Plank was the original founder of the company in 1996, but he stepped down from his leadership role with the company at the end of 2019. He was renamed CEO in April of last year. His goals for his second term include streamlining the business, eliminating non-core product lines, and reducing staff to help reduce costs.

So far, results for the company since his return have been mixed. Before the recent tariff-related selloff, the stock was trading about 9% lower than when he regained his position last year. At one point in November 2024, the stock had gained just over 50%, and UAA was actually ahead of the overall market through February of this year as the chart below shows. After the recent market turbulence, the stock is now down over 20% in the last 12 months. Margins have been improving under Plank, but revenues are still falling, as was seen in the most recent quarterly report.

Under Armour released its Q3 2025 earnings report in February. The company reported a profit of $0.08 per share, beating estimates by $0.05. Revenue of $1.4 billion also exceeded expectations by just over 4%. The company reported improving gross margins and raised its expectations for margin improvement going forward to 160 basis points, compared to the previously projected 125 to 150 basis points. Admittedly, this was all announced before “Liberation Day” last week. It is unclear just how much the new tariffs will impact margins, but the result certainly will not be positive.

The good news from the last earnings report was balanced out by some equally bad news. Revenue has continued to plummet for the company. Q3 revenue numbers were down almost 7% from last year at the same time. The company’s top line is expected to decline by 10% for the full year. This number includes a decline of 12 to 13% in the North American markets. Operating losses for the year are projected to be $0.48 to $0.50. Despite Plank’s best efforts, turning things around at Under Armour will not be a quick and easy process.

So, what is the case for buying UA stock now, especially in light of tariffs that seem to hit some of the countries that supply a significant share of UA’s product lines?

The Case for Under Armour

Much of Plank’s work during his first year back in office appears to be clearing out the low-hanging fruit, pushing several large expenses to the front of the line. In the last year, the company has settled a class action lawsuit that resulted in a $261 million expense for litigation costs. In addition, as stated in the company’s last quarterly report, UA’s operating loss in its “Corporate Other non-operating segment” increased by almost $50 million from the same period last year because of an impairment charge related to vacating the company’s previous global headquarters and a number of other restructuring charges.

The focus for the year ahead will be improving margins and trying to turn around the steep decline in sales, both in the US and international markets. The company will try to spend its marketing dollars wisely and focus on higher-end, “premium” products. Under Armour announced on March 25 that it has become an official footwear and glove partner with the NFL. As part of this partnership, NFL players who are sponsored by Under Armour can now wear UA shoes and gloves on the field. According to the press release, the company will also have “access to NFL media platforms, content creators, and distribution channels to further collaborate on content and initiatives for on-field and in-game exposure.”

While the risk certainly exists that the company’s rebound will take longer than expected or even never quite materialize consistently, the company now trades at a level that appears to have factored in a rather pessimistic viewpoint. Margins are improving. The company trades at a price-to-sales ratio of 0.43. The company does get decent grades in a number of other financial metrics on Seeking Alpha. Over 20 analysts cover the company and currently have an average PE ratio of 13 expected for March of next year.

The company is also in relatively good shape from a debt perspective. Long-term debt to equity is just below 30 as the company has more than enough cash to cover the current debt load. Under Armour has also authorized stock buybacks worth $500 million over the next three years.

It’s Gotta Be the Shoes

This month, Under Armour will release its latest shoe, the Echo. Last week, Sport Illustrated referred to the shoe as “the future of footwear”. Yassine Saidi, UA’s Chief Product Officer said this about the company’s latest attempt to take a real bite out of Nike’s dominance in footwear.

“This isn’t a one-and-done drop. It’s the foundation of a long-term strategy to define Under Armour’s presence in modern sportswear.”

Saidi also said the new shoe is a signal that the company is serious about its commitment to a younger demographic “ensuring its products resonate with trend-setting and culture-defining athletes and creators”.

The positive PR for the Echo comes on the heels (no pun intended) of very positive reviews for UA’s new running and golf shoes. Tech Radar described the new running shoe as “a brilliant long-distance shoe for slower runs with plenty of cushioning”. Golf Monthly said the new UA Drive Fade is “a lot of golf shoe for your money” and that UA “has done a superb job balancing price, performance, and comfort here to create a shoe that will suit a wide range of golfers.”

I am not making the argument that shoe sales alone can turn things around for this once-dominant player in the athletic apparel industry, but new product lines and the renewed NFL partnership show that Under Armour has the potential to carve out real market share in a business segment that was its Achilles heel less than a decade ago.

Tariffs and Other Risks to Consider

Last week’s news of Trump’s new tariffs drove down the stock prices for all of the major sports apparel companies. A large chunk of UA’s goods are made in high-tariff countries like Vietnam and Cambodia. However, Adidas and Nike are in the same boat. There are already indications that Vietnam and Cambodia are willing to negotiate with the Trump Administration to lower their tariffs, but there are no guarantees or clear signs of where things could be even a month from now. In November, UA CFO David Bergman said that the company was not expecting any sizable impacts from tariffs at that time. I do not think that he or anyone else could have predicted the news that we all received last week. All this being said, it appears that tariffs will be impacting sales and margins for all athletic apparel companies for the near future. Should UA try to move production to lower-tariff countries, that move will take time.

Even with tariffs in place, the most significant risk to an investment in UA is that there is simply no guarantee that Under Armour can turn things around. The competition from industry leaders like Nike and Adidas, as well as various new players in the athletic apparel and footwear markets, is steep. The company faces an uphill battle to increase sales both in the US and abroad. Consumer trends for fashion can often change faster than companies can adapt, leaving unsold inventory on the books that must be sold at a significant discount.

Other issues to consider would be risks to the supply chain beyond tariffs. The company is constantly working under the threat of potential political conflict, labor issues, or shortages of raw materials.

The Bottom Line

Having written a number of articles for Seeking Alpha in the past, I would say that this is my highest-risk recommendation, but with the added risk comes the potential for greater reward. The stock currently has short interest of 17.5%. Any significant move upward in the stock price could create pressure for shorts to cover their positions.

There are two classes of Under Armour stock. UAA has voting rights. UA does not. There is another class of stock owned by Kevin Plank that gives him control of the company, which means that the voting rights of UAA has little added value. For risk-tolerant investors, I think Under Armour is a buy heading into the fall. Because the voting rights appear to have no meaningful utility at the current time, the UA shares appear to provide the most compelling value, as they are trading at a slight discount to UAA shares at the time of writing this article.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

data.title: VYM: High Yield Still Yields Less Than Treasuries
data.description:
In my last articles about US-focused ETFs, I highlighted that valuations are reaching extremes and for the first time in my 20 years of investing, I started to ring the alarm bells. Since then, markets have corrected somewhat, but valuations still seem elevated.

A good strategy to circumvent the risks could be to search for undervalued stocks trading at low multiples and high dividend yields. Labeled as a large value fund, the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VYM) is designed to invest in stocks that pay higher dividends than average.

In this article, we're going to dive deeper into the largest holdings to determine how much value there really is in VYM. But first, let's start with a quick overview of where we stand when it comes to valuations in general.

We're in the Upper Range of Valuations Historically

After the recent selloff, the S&P 500 is trading at 25x last year's earnings. When I started to warn about the historical overvaluation, we were closer to 30x earnings. While multiples have come down somewhat, we're still nowhere near fair value.

The S&P 500 traded at an average of 16x earnings for the last century and longer, therefore the average stock is still trading at a 56% premium to its average historical valuation. At this point, some readers might suggest P/E ratios around 25–30 are the new normal, as valuations have been elevated for some time now. I disagree, as market cycles tend to be lengthy.

For example, a historical low for valuations was set at the end of the 1940s, when the average stock traded at just 6-7x earnings. It took well over a decade to reach its heights at over 20x earnings in the 1960s, before it took a nosedive in the 1970s and reached its low in the early 1980s at just 7x earnings. Valuation multiples just like investor sentiment are cyclical. And whenever stocks are trading at lows, investors don't have much interest in them. Yet that's the best time to pick them up for high expected returns at relatively low risks.

The stock market is the only market where things go on sale and all the customers run out of the store.

In other words, there will be decades of low valuations and there will be decades of high valuations. Times of greed are followed by times of fear, just like the night follows the day. The one constant in life is that human psychology never changes. How quickly greed can turn to fear, investors experienced once again after the Trump tariff announcement.

When you're buying stocks at the upper range of valuations, you're exposing yourself to the risk of multiple contraction over the coming decades. That's a risk that investors these days don't seem to focus on extensively, although it could be a massive drag on the future performance of the major indices.

After a 16-year-long bull market that saw the S&P 500 rise by over 700%, we shouldn't bet on further multiple expansion, but prepare for the inevitable return to the mean. Markets seldom trade right at the averages, as investors tend to be either euphoric or depressed. Therefore, we shouldn't rule out that stocks could very well trade below the historical average in the decades to come. That would translate to unsatisfying returns, even if the world economy continues growing at historical averages.

Also, we shouldn't forget that the last few decades were fueled by easy money policies, that saw overall debt levels of companies and countries rise substantially. The final outcome of this economic experiment isn't visible yet, but it could very well have negative effects on growth in the decades to come.

We won't focus on political discussions in this article, but erratic tariff policies that defy any economic sense, could very well be another risk factor in the years ahead. Multiple contraction is a real risk that should be considered and factored in by rational investors.

While stock prices in the short term might recover from here, as they often do after a steep decline in just a few days, I'd be very cautious about buying heavily into it for the long term. The next decade could disappoint based on valuations alone, and this assumption doesn't even include potential political turmoil and global tariff wars.

Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM)

Let's put the doom and gloom aside for a moment, and focus on what you came here for, the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF. The ETF focuses on companies that trade above the average dividend yield. As a value investor, I view this as a plus, but the dividend yield of the S&P 500 sits at just 1.48%, so it shouldn't be too difficult to beat it.

VYM yields 2.86% right now, while that's higher than the S&P 500's average, I wouldn't call it high yield. Yet in today's exuberant markets, that's what gets labeled as a high-yield ETF. Valuations have to come way down in order to make high-yield great again.

With an expense ratio of just 0.06% and 529 holdings, it's cost-effective as well as diversified. Vanguard claims the P/E ratio sits at 19.8x, while Wall Street Journal calculated the P/E ratio at 25.83x. Just by looking at the valuations of the largest holdings, the latter might be the correct number.

Vanguard probably refers to forward earnings, but this metric could be useless in a world of ever-increasing tariff turmoil. I've not once seen analysts rightly predict major turning points. The longer a bull market goes on, the more bullish they get. Just like the crowd that follows their recommendations.

Almost 26x earnings for the average stock is on the upper range of valuations, in other words, I view it as overvalued. The historical average has been 16x over the last century and as VYM is labeled as a large value ETF, I'd like to see the P/E ratio lower.

Sector Weightings

Financial services, technology and consumer defensive sectors top the list. Having 23.02% of the assets invested in financial services seems to make sense for an ETF that focuses on above-average dividend yields, but I view it risky nonetheless.

Financials tend to underperform during a recession and after a 16-years-long bull market that took the markets to lofty heights, it might be a good time to start thinking defensive. Technology is the second-largest sector, which is surprising, since tech companies often come with low dividend yields, if they pay any dividend at all.

Consumer defensive is the third-largest sector. Sadly, most consumer staple companies these days trade at valuations similar to the S&P 500. Therefore, they might not be as defensive as investors might hope for during the next downturn.

Top 10 Holdings

The largest ten holdings represent 25.77% of total assets, that's less concentrated than most other ETFs I analyzed so far. However, for a fund that holds more than 500 stocks, I still would like to see it more diversified.

Broadcom Inc (AVGO)

The largest holding is Broadcom Inc. After the recent selloff, the company is trading at a 35x P/FCF. While that's lower than the 45x the last time I've written about it, I still view it as overvalued.

There's no doubt in my mind, that AI will shape the world in the years and decades to come. However, I am not sure how this emerging industry will possibly achieve a decent ROIC on its sky-high investments in the future. I, as a consumer, wouldn't pay more than $10 or $20 a month to have access to AI and given that there's so much competition, it's reasonable to expect that the new technology will remain free to use for quite some time.

The Chinese company Alibaba (BABA) called the current AI investments in the US as a bubble. While I don't agree with everything that comes out of China, I think they're right. The AI boom could end abruptly in the next recession, when there's less capital available for moonshot ventures. To quote from my previous article about an ETF that included Broadcom:

AI is still in its infancy, let's call it the gold rush phase.

The AI boom could slow down substantially during the next recession, or if the players find out that it isn't as profitable as they once hoped it was.

Whenever there are revolutionary technologies showing up, like the internet, railroads, canals, and now AI, there's usually an initial investing boom where the crowd gets excited. This is usually followed by a huge correction, like we saw during 2000-2003 after the dot-com exuberance.

If you're really a believer in AI, the next large crash will be the opportunity to load up on your favorite shares. But having experienced the markets long enough, whenever prices are low, there just aren't that many believers around.

I'd be very cautious about paying sky-high P/E ratios for the cyclical semiconductor industry right now. While growth rates have been impressive so far, earnings growth could falter in the years to come.

VYM having almost 5% of the assets invested in Broadcom is disappointing, especially since it's labeled as a value ETF that focuses on high dividend yields. Even after the latest decline, Broadcom only yields 1.44%.

Walmart (WMT)

Walmart is a fine company. We all know it, and many of us shop there regularly. It comes with a wide moat and a defensive business model. But to view WMT as a growth stock seems optimistic, given the sheer size of the company. Yet, Mr. Market has decided it deserves a 35x P/E ratio. I disagree.

Growth rates for the last 10 years have been 5% a year, while growth has accelerated in recent years, I am skeptical a large company like Walmart can continue to grow in the double-digits. Yet if you're paying 35x earnings, the company better keeps growing rapidly. Otherwise you're just overpaying.

The underlying business saw very little growth since 2014, buybacks helped to accelerate the growth rates, but this widely used tool of financial engineering becomes less powerful, if your stock is trading at 35x earnings.

My best guess is the next bear market, stocks like WMT will face a painful reality check. WMT dividend yield sits at 1.12% right now, and it remains a mystery to me how VYM makes it its fourth-largest holding, since the ETF focuses on above-average dividend yields and value. WMT isn't good value and the dividend yield is below the S&P 500 average, which also remains near its historical lows.

Procter & Gamble (PG)

Procter & Gamble is my idea of a good business, as it produces generous cash flows year after year. It's relatively recession-resistant, as it provides essential products.

However, the company faces similar issues as its peers in the consumer staple sector. Competition from lower-priced private labels is heating up, as inflation took the price of many products too far for its loyal customer base.

Growth rates for consumer staples can't be considered dynamic, and in order to generate great returns with a stock like PG, we need to make sure we pay a fair price. Right now, the stock is trading at 25x earnings, which seems optimistic for a company with single-digit growth rates.

The dividend yield at 2.53% is well below the yield of treasuries, and you're facing all the risks that comes with investing in individual companies. While I like the company, I don't like the stock at current valuations. And that's the issue I have with many US stocks these days. The underlying businesses are great, but the valuations just got out of hand. Even after the recent sell-off we're nowhere near fair value.

VYM Comparison Against Peers

Let's end this article with a quick comparison against other value ETFs and some closing words.

When it comes to stock price appreciation, VYM has performed well over the last 5 years. It's been difficult for any ETF focused on US stocks not to perform greatly over the last decade or longer. The outperformance partly relied on multiple expansion, and since we're already in the upper range of historical valuations, I wouldn't bet on this trend continuing.

After the recent steep selloff, it's prudent to expect some form of short-term recovery, as the buy-the-dip-crowd will likely step in. However, for the long term I still view the current valuations as risky, and I therefore rate VYM a "hold".

This rating is not based on the quality of the underlying businesses, but on the overall exuberant market environment we're in. Politicians around the world are trying their best to make high-yield great again, and I'm confident we can get there in the years ahead.

data.title: A Test Of Endurance Is Underway As Trump Narrows Trade War Focus On China
data.description:
By Lynn Song

Tariff escalation abruptly sends trade friction into the endgame

As my colleagues have covered in an earlier piece, Trump's latest sledgehammer tariff reversal was "not just chaotic, but crazy." In most of the world, investors likely were able to breathe a sigh of relief, but in China, no such tariff reprieve is on the table quite yet.

Markets were hit hard by the reciprocal tariff round, but have had smaller reactions to further escalations since. A big reason for this is that we have already moved into the endgame for tariffs, where further tariff hikes are less and less meaningful.

Our broad take on how this process has unfolded is below:

  • At the lower end of tariffs, suppliers, importers, and customers end up sharing part of the burden and ultimately, trade doesn’t take too much of a hit. This is largely what was expected when tariffs were hiked around 10% or so, during the first round of fentanyl tariffs.
  • As tariffs are hiked further, there's no more room for suppliers or importers to cut their margins, and further costs will have to be fully passed on to the consumer. Products with viable substitutions are swapped out to different suppliers and trade starts to plummet. This is where the biggest impact on trade and growth tends to occur, as each incremental tariff hike renders more and more trade non-viable. We likely started moving into this territory by the second fentanyl tariff hike and went well into this territory with the reciprocal tariff round.
  • Past a certain point, hiking tariffs further doesn't do too much more damage to exporters, who have already likely been phased out if alternatives existed. Instead, importers could end up taking more of the burden; importers were already not going to buy the product if there were any viable alternatives, but would either still be forced to buy it if there were no substitutes, or cease that part of its operations altogether if tariffs made their business commercially non-viable. It's very likely that the subsequent escalation of tariffs has sent us into this territory already for numerous products.

Direct US-China trade will likely crater starting with the April data, but certainly won't disappear overnight. During the first trade war, a lot of China’s price sensitive exports were already redirected or cut. It’s hard to say exactly how much, but a sizeable portion of exports don’t have a good substitution, nor is there an easy way to simply produce specific products elsewhere on short notice. Trump's tariff reprieve will keep re-export channels open for at least another quarter, and we suspect that this round of tariff shocks could lead to many importers frontloading imports again with more urgency this time around.

Moving forward, China is likely to respond again, as it has already vowed to fight to the end. It's certainly possible both countries will get stuck in a continued loop of tariff hikes, but markets will likely start to phase these out. More meaningful escalations to watch for could take the form of expanded export or import controls, or measures to hit companies in terms of market access.

We expect a test of endurance to see who comes to the bargaining table at a disadvantage

Trump's backpedaling from global tariffs but escalation of tariffs on China at first glance feels like a bad development for China, as a wider tariff gap between the rates levied on China and the rest of the world on paper should increase the amount of viable substitution products.

However, despite these rapid escalations, our view is that China has averted the worst-case scenario. The worst-case scenario isn't infinity percent tariffs from the US, but rather a scenario where the US successfully leveraged tariff threats to coerce other countries into coordinated tariff action against China, which the Trump administration reportedly requested Mexico to do earlier this year. While we did indeed see many countries immediately try to start negotiations – as Trump himself mocked country leaders as saying "Please, please sir, make a deal. I'll do anything. I'll do anything, sir" along with other vulgarities at a Republican dinner event – it's possible that the EU's moves to formalise retaliation could've signalled that the world was not going to simply capitulate.

We see some voices in markets speculating that these rounds of tariffs mark a point of no return for US-China decoupling. We don't think this is the case – both sides have signalled to varying extents that they’d prefer negotiations, but the problem is that neither party wants to be seen as backing down.

Our view is there is a bit of a culture clash, which further exacerbates the friction. Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs were a textbook case of negotiation anchoring, the theory where an aggressive opening offer leads to a more advantageous position for negotiations. US Treasury Secretary Bessent's comments after the tariff announcements discouraging retaliation and encouraging dialogue show the likely intention was to use these tariffs to force countries to the table. However, in China, there is a huge importance attached to mutual respect and negotiating as equals, as well as a strong importance attached to face.

As such, Trump's unilateral tariff hikes and years of anti-China rhetoric have added barriers to negotiations. The way that countries which have tried to negotiate have been treated so far this year also certainly has not done much to encourage China to sit down at the table as well. At this point, politics certainly appears to be trumping economics so no one can say with confidence when and how talks could resume, but for now, it looks like policymakers are willing to put economic theory to the test to see who ultimately feels the pain to see who has the edge when talks resume.

Tariff spiral shows why intentional currency devaluation was never a viable option

There have been many voices in the markets interpreting the People's Bank of China's weakening of the CNY fixing as a sign of devaluation in the face of tariff pressure, but this argument does not stack up. The moves higher have been very gradual, and certainly won't move the needle in the face of 125% tariffs. Instead, it is likely a managed response to the outflow pressure in light of last week's developments, and still shows a commitment toward managing currency stability.

The developments from the past week illustrate very clearly why we have been saying for the past half year that intentional CNY depreciation to offset tariffs was a heavily flawed argument – if China was truly planning to rely on devaluation to help offset tariffs, the CNY would need a truly significant devaluation in order to do so, and such a move could again easily be countered by further tariff hikes from the Trump administration. At most, a shock devaluation would only buy a very small window of time for exporters.

Furthermore, the damage of such a shock devaluation move to domestic purchasing power, market sentiment, and China's RMB internationalisation plans would far outweigh the benefit to trade. In an era of protectionism as well as economic transition where China is increasingly moving parts of its manufacturing abroad with domestic production increasingly focused on up the value added ladder, China's outward investments will continue to grow, and a stronger and stable CNY will help support these investments.

We’re holding our baseline USDCNY fluctuation band at 7-7.40 for this year, and we expect the greatest pressure on the top end of this band to be in the second quarter. If this level does break, our bear case scenario has 7.50 as a ceiling for the year.

PBOC continues tight grip on currency stabilisation

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold: Systemic Bonanza Awaits
data.description:
Nixon “closed” gold redemption in August 1971 after France sent a battleship to collect its gold from the USA. France rightly worried that the USA was printing far more US$ than it could cover in gold and asked for their gold, returning the US$ held.

“In August 1971, French president Pompidou sent a battleship to New York harbor to remove France’s gold from the vault of the New York Federal Reserve Bank and to transport it to the Banque de France in Paris. Soon thereafter, gold accounted for 92 percent of French reserves. On August 11, the British requested that the Treasury remove the $3 billion of gold from the U.S. depository of Fort Knox to the New York Federal Reserve vault, where the gold of foreign governments was stored. … When Nixon spoke on August 15, 1971, the U.S. held less than 10,000 tons of gold, less than half of what it once had.”

A "Barbarous Relic": The French, Gold, and the Demise of Bretton Woods

(Citation: Michael J. Graetz & Olivia Briffault, A "Barbarous Relic": The French, Gold, and the Demise of Bretton Woods, THE BRETTON WOODS AGREEMENTS, TOGETHER WITH SCHOLARLY COMMENTARIES AND ESSENTIAL HISTORICAL DOCUMENTS, NAOMI LAMOREAUX & IAN SHAPIRO (EDS.), YALE UNIVERSITY PRESS, 2019; YALE LAW & ECONOMICS RESEARCH PAPER NO. 558; COLUMBIA LAW & ECONOMICS WORKING PAPER NO. 560 (2016).)

It was just a small batch as France had already repatriated 3, 313 tonnes of gold from 1963 to 1966.

“All France’s dollars were converted into gold, and to avoid treachery, the metal was repatriated over the course of three years. It took 44 boat trips and 129 flights to bring home more than three thousand tonnes of gold to the Banque de France in Paris.”

How France Secretly Repatriated All Its Gold Before Nixon's Dollar Devaluation

One must surmise from the language used that “trust” had left the room by then. Clearly, many other countries were doing the same with more than 10,000 tonnes having been withdrawn.

At the time of the 1971 French withdrawal, other countries were once again lining up to do the same and Nixon, in a panic, seeing how all that gold would flow out of the USA “closed the gold window” on a Sunday evening. Spin for “we will no longer redeem dollars for gold as we agreed at Bretton Woods in 1944”, essentially resiling from a commitment. It simply left all the remaining countries high and dry holding fiat $ no longer backed by gold. Their expected gold redemption was gone.

The current global fiat monetary system was born on that 15th August 1971. It had undergone a large number of additional refinements since then, like the development of the Risk Weighted bank credit system and a myriad of new central bank “tools” to push fiat money into the financial system. I’ve discussed the system in my article last month, so, please catch up there if you need the background (link below).

When I wrote an article last month about the severe systemic risk prevailing right now in the international financial system,” Global Financial Markets: Slow Motion Crash Underway”, I could not offer any timing, still can’t. The speed, however, of the market declines since then took me a bit by surprise as I expected a decline over time in a directional change.

Removing gold redemption took all the countries by surprise, and they literally had no idea what to do about it, so they did nothing and just continued to trade in US$ as if it was still backed by gold. I had been told by old-timer bankers that they were at loss to make international payments or trade in foreign currencies with no advice for the central bank or government in the weeks after that 1971 event. Thus, the global fiat monetary system was born out of default and accepted politically for the sake of expediency. It was not even subjected to any designs and scrutiny.

It funded the USA twin deficits, endless deficit spending and trade balance deficits since 1971. Many academic papers followed praising the abandonment of the gold standard often quoting Keynes’ 1924 dismissal of gold as a “barbaric relic”. I recall seeing editorial cartoons of Presidents walking tall after breaking the chains of the gold standard in 1971. Now we see editorial cartoons of toilet rolls as replacement for the gold standard. You can even buy them as novelty items.

The process to gain acceptance for paper money begins by fully backing it with gold redemption. Then let all the little froggies get used to paper money and slowly cook away gold redemption. It's almost childlike in simplicity, but the inevitable result of fiat money is that governments will create more-and-more fiat until nobody “trust” in it anymore. So here we are again with that word, “trust”.

Germany, held 1,500 tonnes of gold with the US Federal Reserve but in 2013 started a process of repatriating some of that gold (and some held in France), aiming to repatriate 674 tonnes (300 tonnes from the USA) back to the Bundesbank. It was reported two days ago, in the German tabloid Bild that Germany now wants to repatriate the remaining 1,200 tonnes of gold held in the USA. It’s all mixed up in current politics. The “trust” is once again gone. Countries asked, where is our gold, we want it and Nixon “closed the gold window”. German politicians now ask the same, show us the gold, or better yet, return our gold.

“German tabloid Bild, owned by POLITICO parent company Axel Springer, reported on Thursday that outgoing Christian Democratic Union (CDU) lawmaker Marco Wanderwitz is one of those concerned.

Already in 2012, Wanderwitz had unsuccessfully requested to inspect the gold holdings as part of an effort to pressure the bank to either take a more active role as a custodian, or to repatriate it to Germany.”

Where’s the gold? Germany’s conservatives sound the alarm over reserves in the US, Politico.

Nobody took Wanderwitz serious in 2012, but now the political climate fosters mistrust in a global fiat financial system entirely dependent upon trust. The thought that Germany or other countries be denied their gold by closing yet another gold window suddenly does not sound all that preposterous. What’s to stop a decree that no gold will be allowed to be exported out of the USA. Historical precedent already exist when Roosevelt did exactly that in 1933.

Roosevelt’s decree also confiscated, to the government, all the gold held by anybody in the USA and USA citizens were not allowed to own gold.

“I, Franklin D. Roosevelt, President of the United States of America, do declare that said national emergency still continues to exist and pursuant to said section do hereby prohibit the hoarding of gold coin, gold bullion, and gold certificates within the continental United States by individuals, partnerships, associations and corporations…”

Executive Order 6102—Forbidding the Hoarding of Gold Coin, Gold Bullion and Gold Certificates, April 05,1933.

Let’s turn to the aftermath of August 1971.

President Gerald Ford passed a law allowing US citizens to once again own gold in December 1974.

Pub. L. 93-373

“An Act to provide for increased participation by the United States in the International Development Association and to permit United States citizens to purchase, hold, sell, or otherwise deal with gold in the United States or abroad.”

We have an overlap period between 1971 and 1974 where US citizens were still not allowed to own gold, and it is generally accepted that no free-market gold price could exist in the period from 1933 to 1974, at least, though I believe one should only time series the gold price from 1976 as a market price.

Source: Macrotrends.net

The regulated price was at an average of $42.71 for August 1971 and gold would go into a bull run peaking at $850 in 1980 before falling back into the $350 range. The introduction of a fiat monetary system, a free-market gold price, and unrestricted ownership in the USA were systemic events, the global rules changed. The gold price increased around 19 times (1900%) the price it was in 1980. What would the gold price do if we had another monetary systemic event where the fiat monetary system fails on trust and gold makes a comeback? An19-times increase in the gold price would move it from around $3,000 now to $57,000. Looks absurd, yet that is what happened after the 1971 systemic event. Imagine suggesting a $850 gold price to people in 1974.

I am loath to use an inflation adjusted gold price chart, as reported inflation is netted inflation. It is often remarked that there was hardly any inflation in the roaring 20’s yet the markets collapsed in 1929.

The Fed of the 1920s had no qualms about punishing the printing press.

(in billions of dollars) St Louis Fed.

Here we can see the M3 money supply went from $19.31 billion in 1913, the year of the creation of the USA Federal Reserve, to $55.20 billion in 1929. Up 186% over 16 years.

Let’s just compare the M3 for the USA from Nov 2007 to Nov 2023, 16 years. I know, it's irrational as we had 2008, and we had COVID, but let’s anyway. Nov 2007 M3 = $7.4 trillion. Nov 2023 = $20.7 trillion. Well, what do you know, it's up only 180%! The freshly minted Fed of the 1920s did not need a global financial crisis and a COVID to overheat the printing press and even outprinted a 2008/COVID Fed, so why no inflation? The answer is found in productivity advances.

Digging up these old data sets is a challenge and seldom exactly what one would prefer. Here is productivity data from for the period 1913 to 1929, but it's mixed up with other data. The 1901 – 1919 includes WW1. The roaring 20s gave us TFP (total factor productivity) of 2%.

Source: Technological Breakthroughs and Productivity Growth, Research Institute of Industrial Economics, IFN Working Paper No. 665, 2006

Comparing it to the published data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics for the period 2004 to 2024 (the red bars) and we see that a 2% average for TFP over a 10-year period is extraordinary even by today’s standards. The main reason was the electrification of everything and all the advances that flowed from that. Other technologies such as the telephone, mass media, motor vehicles, air conditioning and many more contributed. It was a high advancement period for humanity.

Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics

Here's the thing about increases in TFP, it measures how we are making economic progress done in "large data", an average over all of the economy. Inventions, technology, systems, practices, protocols, techniques and many more factors contribute to our economic advancement and here we measure how we improved. The bottom line is that these economic advances are deflationary. Yes, that evil deflation which you should avoid at all costs. We make a better product, cheaper, more cost-efficient, more automated manufacture, more scale for economies of scale, with innovation and new technologies and the effect on general prices is that they must decline, deflate.

Well then, where is all that deflation, all those lower prices? All the benefits from human advancement have been swept away with money creation plus another 2% on top, and a lot more when it went over the 2%. When we do an inflation adjusted gold price, we need to also add back the hidden part of the inflation which was done to negate the deflation of human advancement (think mobile phones over the past 10 years for example or PCs in the 1990s), or the data otherwise could significantly underestimate the inflation adjustment required for gold. I doubt an inflation adjusted gold price would be of much assistance when we go into a monetary system crisis of trust.

Modern Fed could create money at an 180% pace over 16 years with only about 1% average TFP increase, keeping it even under the 2% target until they went over the top on COVID stimulus. The 1920s Fed had an average 2% TFP increase to work with, and inflation seldom appeared.

Short-term “correction”, even a brutal one, vs a Systemic Crisis.

Markets have become used to being bailed out. Just buy the dip financial advisors preach. All will blow over in no time, do not panic. It’s all banking on a Fed market intervention like in 2008 or during COVID. High volatility does not inspire trust.

What is different today? The Fed has overburdened its balance sheet with directly purchased government debt and is literally making losses larger than its capital, which technically makes it insolvent until it monetizes itself out of the hole, i.e. create money to bail itself out. The Fed must dig itself out of the hole carefully, for inflation cannot be reignited. The inflation is only half the problem, the real problem is interest rates will then increase and wipe out the majority of government tax income. Then government will have to explain to voters why they must pay higher taxes and suffer inflation concurrently. There is no big bang bazooka bailout for itself let alone to bail out the global financial markets without serious consequences this time around.

The Fed talk currently is about “uncertainty”, tariffs may cause inflation, room to cut interest rates is limited, etc. No bazooka talk yet. It is, however, certain that a monetary bazooka in the current market will feed directly into renewed inflation and add to interest rates going up, which, in turn, will increase the Fed’s losses. It’s a very muted Fed when the USA markets alone have already taken a $10 trillion hit. Who is nursing those losses, waiting for a bailout, waiting for the customary bounce?

The losses will vest very quickly in the absence of a bailout, and systemic distrust will make Germany of us all. Where is my gold, I want it in my possession. All of it.

I do not believe this is going to be just a short-term correction. The systemic signals of central banks accumulating gold reserves, Germany openly discussing collecting their gold, all 1,200 tonnes of it from the USA, the gold price in a strong bull phase, and gold being discussed as a portfolio choice when it was until recently considered a no-interest earning drag on a portfolio. Germany will probably need a fleet of battleships to take it 1200 tonnes home. General global mistrust between “friends” now exist, even before a crisis, what happens if/when the system unravels, and adults need to manage the rapids?

Wanderwitz asked to inspect the German gold and was refused. It fueled rumors that the gold may not be there, may have been leased and sold, may have multiple owners. Trust vs mistrust. Mistrust will not go away until it is proven unfounded.

Gold’s role as benchmark global currency of intrinsic value has not changed. Gold is the sanctuary benchmark for value when fiat crumbles. It is not a barbaric relic, as Keynes has claimed. Neither should one have greater faith in fiat dollars as a store of value when the first systemic tremors appear. Systems trust has already left the room. Governments may force their own populations to stick with their fiat, but it would not be possible to force countries to accept a fiat they do not trust. A tyrannical Zimbabwe tried to force their hyperinflated fiat on their population and it failed.

People have always gone back to gold when the fiat systems failed. The physical gold is already being moved to own hands. Having gold this time will not be different from pervious fiat failures, have you got yours?

data.title: PDI: A Golden Buying Opportunity
data.description:
PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI) crashed 10% on Friday after investors went into a complete and utter tariff panic, driving major stock indices sharply lower. The reason: the U.S. government announced new tariffs on a large number of countries last week. However, yield and income-driven investments should continue to do well in 2025, in my opinion, and the current drop creates a truly unique buying opportunity for investors looking to buy into this closed-end fund at an attractive valuation. The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has generated double-digit annual total returns since its inception in 2012 and with the Federal Reserve likely going to cut federal fund rates in 2025, the CEF's fixed income investments could reasonably re-price to the upside.

Previous Rating

I rated PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund a strong buy in September 2024 -- PDI: 13% Yield And Revaluation Potential -- because I believed the closed-end fund would benefit from lower interest rates that the Federal Reserve began to lower starting in September 2024. In the fourth-quarter of last year, the Federal Reserve did lower federal fund rates, by a total of 100 basis points, but it has since taken it slowly with rate cuts, in part due to resurgent inflation toward the end of 2024 as well as in January 2024. With markets crashing and a recession at least a growing probability, I believe it has become much more likely that the Federal Reserve will lower interest rates this year. Since the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is a fixed income fund whose assets are set to benefit from lower federal fund rates, I believe PDI is a highly attractive investment option for investors currently.

Massive Market Turmoil Creates Engagement Opportunity for Dividend Investors

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is a fixed income-focused, closed-end fund [CEF] and manages approximately $6.3B in fund net assets. The CEF is mainly focused on a small number of fixed income asset classes such as non-agency mortgages and high-yield credit. Other asset classes include emerging market debt, commercial mortgage-backed securities, as well as government and developed market bonds.

The largest asset class in PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is non-agency mortgages which represent 25.2% of total fund assets, while the second-largest asset group is high-yield credit which has a representation of 24.7%. The closed-end fund is chiefly oriented toward U.S. investments, with approximately 70% of all fund assets invested in U.S. denominated investment securities.

This is what PDI's portfolio currently looks like:

The main reason why I believe the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is attractive as an income investment on the drop is that PDI is facing a significant re-pricing catalyst in its portfolio in 2025. As the portfolio consists of fixed income instruments whose values are negatively correlated with interest rates, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund could see a catalyst for net asset value growth this year.

Bond prices tend to move in the opposite direction as interest rates, which means bonds (and other fixed income instruments) are preferred investments for investors when interest rates are expected to peak... which already happened last year. As fixed income investments become more attractive during periods when the Federal Reserve cuts federal fund rates (which is more likely than not to happen in 2025), the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is in an excellent buying setup right now. While the PDI pays a very healthy yield, the underlying fixed income instruments are set for a re-pricing, which could lead to a higher net asset value for the closed-end fund.

Additionally, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has returned attractive total returns since it was started in May 2012: the total annual return per year is 10.87% and the closed-end fund has distributed regular cash for a very long time as well.

Investors Can Establish a Long-Term Position in PDI at an Above-Average, Near 15% Yield

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is trading significantly above its longer-term dividend yield, which is another indication that investors may have become a little bit too bearish on stocks and income-oriented funds such as the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund. Currently, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund yields 15.6% which is almost 2 PP above the 1-year average dividend yield.

PDI's Valuation

The premium for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund value massively shrunk last week as investors panicked and sold shares into the weakness. The exaggerated market reaction creates a unique setup for income-oriented, cashed-up buyers, in my opinion: the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has previously traded at a double-digit premium to NAV, which has now been almost completely eradicated.

Investors still pay a premium of about 2.7% to NAV, but this premium is significantly lower (by 13 PP) compared to PDI's pre-crash valuation The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's net asset value premium is now also 6PP below the 3-year average NAV premium, meaning investors seem to be getting a really good deal right now. The last time, the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund was this cheap was in Q4'23.

Risks With PDI

The single biggest reason to own the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund relates to the potential for federal fund rate cuts in FY 2025, which is something I believe we are going to see in the second half of the year... especially if Federal Reserve bankers see higher risks for a potential recession. The biggest downside risk here is that the Federal Reserve won't cut federal fund rates in 2025, which means the CEF's fixed income instruments won't benefit from a re-pricing catalyst.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, the 10% drop last Friday is totally exaggerated and gives dividend investors the opportunity to snatch a more than 15% yield out of the bargain bin. The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund is set to benefit from lower federal fund rates, which may manifest in the second half of the year. With or without a recession, interest rates are set to decline in the long term, which will benefit PDI. The closed end fund's portfolio consists of rate-sensitive fixed income instruments which generate predictable income for investors. With investors now also paying a much lower premium to NAV, I believe the PDI currently has the best buying setup in about two years.

data.title: Apple: Better Now, But Don't Buy
data.description:
Article Thesis

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has been extremely volatile in recent weeks, and while shares soared on Wednesday, they trade well below the highs from the end of 2024. While Apple's valuation has come down compared to the highs seen last year, AAPL is still far from a bargain today, and it remains highly exposed to macro threats such as tariffs or an escalating Taiwan conflict.

Past Coverage

I have written about Apple in the past here on Seeking Alpha, most recently in January, around one quarter ago. I gave the company a "Sell" rating back then, which has worked out fine so far, as AAPL is down by around 20% since that article was published. I was bearish mainly due to Apple's very elevated valuation at the time, while macro concerns such as tariffs were not really on the horizon yet. With Apple's valuation coming down and with the macro picture, especially related to trade tensions with China, changing, it is time for an update.

Apple: An Extremely Volatile Giant

Apple is one of the largest companies in the world in terms of market capitalization, and yet it almost trades like a small-cap in the very recent past—its volatility is very elevated. While many investors see Apple's shares as very low-risk and stable, they have been among the weakest performers in the Mag 7 group of stocks this year:

In the above chart, we see that Apple delivered the second-weakest year-to-date return among the Mag 7 stocks, with only Tesla (TSLA) faring worse. Prior to Wednesday's massive market rally, Apple was down by more than 30%, but even following that partial recovery, AAPL is still down by more than 20%.

Apple Is Exposed To Macro Headwinds

In recent days, tariffs have gotten a lot of attention, and rightfully so. After all, the tariffs that were instated by the new US President, Donald Trump, could impact a wide range of companies, as well as consumers, substantially. As of Wednesday afternoon, many tariffs have been postponed for 90 days, but the 10% base tariffs are still in play. Also, importantly, tariffs on China are still in place and have even been increased to a now very high level of 125%, and China's tariffs on the US are in place as well.

Apple, of course, is a US-based company, but that doesn't mean that it is immune to these tariffs. iPhones, as well as some of the other consumer hardware that Apple sells, are manufactured in several countries—one of them is China, so tariffs on China do have an impact on Apple. Even beyond the direct financial impact of tariffs, there could be headwinds: Tariffs could disrupt global trade flow, for example, which could result in supply chain issues for a giant like Apple with its very large and diverse supply chain.

As a company that is seen as very "American", Apple could also be a prime target of reciprocal actions. If the Chinese government were to retaliate against rising US tariffs, they could move against US tech companies, for example, e.g. by putting restrictions on who can buy an iPhone, or what apps can be downloaded on the App Store, and so on.

Europe, with which the US has some trade tensions as well, is another major economy that could move against big US tech companies, including Apple—there has been some talk recently about the EU potentially putting additional restrictions or taxes on US tech companies. The EU could, for example, try to take a larger share of Apple's App Store revenue in Europe via some form of a digital tax.

As a reaction to the US tariffs on many other countries (although some of them have now been postponed), consumers in these countries may also be less willing to buy one of Apple's products. Chinese consumers, for example, could be angered by the very large tariffs the US has imposed against China, which could make Chinese consumers opt for non-Apple products when they buy their next smartphone, notebook, and so on. Some of this sentiment exists in Europe and other markets as well; thus, this could be a headwind outside of China, too—although I would not be surprised if China is the country with the most pronounced impact.

Another risk factor, although a not very likely one, I believe, is an escalating Taiwan conflict. Apple does not only depend on China as an end market for its products and as a manufacturer of its hardware, but Apple is also extremely dependent on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) for chip production. If the Taiwan conflict were to escalate—this is, I believe, unlikely but not impossible—then Apple's supply chains would be disrupted in a massive way.

While there are several macro risks and headwinds for Apple, the company also has some advantages or mitigating factors. Its brand is very strong, and its products are generally in the premium segment, which gives Apple considerable pricing power. Tariff-related cost increases can thus, at least partially, be passed on to consumers, I assume. Apple also has high margins, so if tariff tensions were to result in some margin headwinds, profits would not fall off a cliff. If a company with a net margin of 2% sees its margins drop by 200 base points, all profits would vanish, for example, but Apple with its net margin of around 24% over the last year would be less impacted by some margin deterioration. Also, Apple has a clean balance sheet with a considerable cash position, which helps reduce risks further—in case we get into a recession, Apple has the financial resources to stomach such a situation.

Apple's Valuation: Better, But Still Not Good

When Apple's shares peaked at around $260 at the end of 2024, its valuation was very high—based on current estimates, the company will earn around $7.20 to $7.30 this year; thus, Apple was trading at 36x net earnings a couple of months ago. For a large and established company that is not growing very fast any longer, that wasn't justified at all, I believe.

Following the recent sell-off, Apple is now trading at a little less than $200 per share, which pencils out to an earnings multiple of around 27, which means an earnings yield of 3.6%. An earnings yield like that is not especially attractive compared to treasury yields of more than 4%, but at least the valuation is now not as excessive as it was a couple of months ago.

Looking at Apple's enterprise value to EBITDA ratio, which also accounts for cash and debt on the balance sheet, we see the following:

The multiple has come down to the high teens range and is now around 10% higher than it was, on average, over the last decade. This suggests that right now is still not an especially good time to buy—shares are still somewhat on the expensive side. But right now, shares are trading way closer to the historically normal or fair valuation range than they did a couple of months ago, when the enterprise value to EBITDA multiple was way above 20.

Of course, both the EBITDA estimate for this year and the earnings per share estimate for this year could decline to some degree going forward due to the expected tariff impact, but no matter what, Apple below $200 is not as overvalued as it was way above $200 per share.

Takeaway

Apple has not been a safe haven asset in the recent past, even though many investors see it that way. Shares have been very volatile, and AAPL has underperformed the Mag 7 average so far this year.

The company could be impacted by tariffs and trade tensions in several ways, but at least its valuation has declined, and it looks less overvalued now compared to before the sell-off. I still don't deem it an attractive investment right here, but am less bearish than I was earlier this year.

data.title: The Tariff Pause
data.description:
By Warren Patterson | Ewa Manthey

Energy – Tariff pause provides some relief

Oil prices rallied along with risk assets yesterday after President Trump paused reciprocal tariffs on most trading partners, at least those that haven’t retaliated yet. This puts tariffs back to the baseline 10%. However, there was no relief for China. The US increased tariffs to 125% after China raised retaliatory tariffs on US goods to 84%. While the pause offers some relief to markets, there’s still plenty of uncertainty on the trade front. This uncertainty is still likely to drag on global growth, which is clearly a concern for oil demand. Still, conditions are not looking as bad as they were just a few days ago.

The ICE Brent forward curve is signalling a better-supplied oil market, at least across parts of the forward curve. While the front end of the curve is in backwardation, it has shifted into contango from the January 2026 contract onwards. Meanwhile, the Brent Dec-25 - Dec-26 spread has fallen into contango. All signs point to a softer market balance a little bit further down the road.

US inventory data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) shows that crude oil inventories increased by 2.55m barrels over the last week. This takes stocks to a little over 442m barrels -- the highest since July. Meanwhile, crude stocks at Cushing grew by 681k barrels, leaving stocks at the WTI delivery hub at their highest level since November.
